My right eyelid droops a little and the pupil looks smaller during an attack
I get sweaty on the right side of my face during the pain
Bright light and loud sounds bother me a little, but I’m not usually nauseated

I’ve noticed small muscle twitching in my eyelid/cheek when I’m exhausted or after an attack

I’ve used leftover strong pain pills from a past dental issue because I felt desperate

I’ve been worried it could be something like an aneurysm or brain tumor because it’s so intense and always on one side
Sometimes my jaw feels tight on that side, so I wondered if it was a tooth problem or TMJ

Heartburn after spicy foods
Seasonal sneezing in the spring
A mild stiff neck after long hours at the computer

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male in IT support. Start with: “I keep getting brutal one-sided headaches behind my right eye that hit out of nowhere.” Volunteer: severe stabbing right retro-orbital pain in waves; you can’t sit still (pacing/rocking); right eye red/tearing; right nostril stuffy/runny. If asked, add: clusters (daily for weeks then months off), 45–120 min, often wake you 1–2 hours after sleep, same times each day; right eyelid droop/smaller pupil; right facial sweating; mild light/sound sensitivity, usually no nausea; alcohol triggers during bad stretches; OTC pain meds barely help; smoking makes it worse; eyelid/cheek twitching when exhausted/after attacks; days of restlessness before a cluster.
Withhold unless directly asked about safety/substances: scary thoughts “I can’t live with this” but no plan/intent; you used leftover strong dental pain pills once or twice when desperate.
Express misconceptions/worries: “Maybe sinus infection/migraine/TMJ/tooth,” fear aneurysm/tumor; ask if you need repeated scans. Emphasize cost concerns about oxygen/injections, ER bills, and preventives needing monitoring.
If the clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly, thank them, but remain worried about affordability and safety.

medications: Edaravone 60 mg IV once daily (intermittent courses)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
Melatonin 10 mg PO nightly
confounders: Family history of cluster headache (father and paternal uncle) increasing diagnostic anchoring
Current smoker and recent alcohol use; alcohol triggers can mimic episodic pattern and complicate history
Intermittent unilateral nasal congestion and tearing during attacks can be misattributed to sinusitis/allergic rhinitis
Fasciculations likely exacerbated by hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia and stimulant use (energy drinks), raising concern for neuromuscular disease
Elevated blood pressure and tachycardia during severe pain may be mistaken for primary hypertension or panic attacks

required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain
Short attacks (15-180 minutes) with recurrence
Ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation/conjunctival injection/rhinorrhea/ptosis)

medications: Oxygen 100% nonrebreather mask 12-15 L/min inhalation for 15-20 minutes at onset

Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily; titrate every 1-2 weeks as tolerated to 240-480 mg/day
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2 days
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period
Regular sleep schedule
Headache diary and trigger tracking
Neurology referral for preventive management and consideration of CGRP monoclonal antibody or occipital nerve block if refractory

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and focus on: “She gets sudden high fevers out of nowhere every few weeks, lasting 2–4 days, and she’s not herself.” Volunteer that during fevers she’s cranky, very tired, won’t eat, sugars run very high, and you fear a seizure because she’s had seizures before.

Only if asked, add belly pain/vomiting, occasional loose stools, a red blotchy rash with fevers, mouth sores, swollen neck glands, joint aches/doesn’t want to walk, poor growth, and small head size.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: darker/foamy urine after episodes, peeing less when sick, frequent infections triggering episodes, fruity breath when sugars high, or that vaccines/stress/missed sleep can worsen it.

If asked about diabetes care, admit reluctantly: sometimes you skip insulin when vomiting; you’ve used leftover antibiotics; you delayed care fearing judgment.

Push a kidney/nephritis idea (back/side pain, puffy eyes, home dipstick “protein”). Worry about costs and “wasting” glargine.

If clinician shows empathy, soften, share more, and ask what to do during the next fever.

Explain treatment pathway including surgery and endocrinology involvement, and discuss observation with serial MRI only if asymptomatic and low-risk (as determined by specialist).
Safety-net and follow-up recommendations.

patient.chief_complaint: My fingers and wrists have been aching and stiff, and my ring finger keeps kind of catching when I try to bend it.

volunteer: Morning stiffness in both hands that takes over an hour to loosen up
Swelling and soreness in both wrists and several finger knuckles
A finger that clicks/catches when bending, sometimes painful at the palm
Trouble opening jars and turning keys because my hands feel weak and sore
if_asked: The finger catching is worse first thing in the morning and after using my hands a lot
Tenderness along the palm side of the fingers and wrist, like the tendons are sore
Warmth in the small joints of my hands during flare-ups
Pain in the balls of my feet when I first stand up
Feeling unusually tired for weeks, even after a full night’s sleep
Shortness of breath when walking up a flight of stairs, worse than my usual baseline
hidden: New numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers at night that sometimes wakes me up
Dropping objects more often because my grip gives out suddenly
Dry, gritty feeling in the eyes and a dry mouth that makes it hard to swallow crackers
Small, firm bumps near the elbows that don’t hurt
A tight, uncomfortable feeling in the chest with exertion that improves with rest (patient thinks it’s just being out of shape)
resistant: Needing to stop to catch my breath during sex and feeling embarrassed about it
Using more of my leftover pain pills than prescribed on bad days because I don’t want to bother anyone
misleading: I think it might be carpal tunnel from typing so much at work
I wondered if it’s gout because the joints get really sore and swollen
I thought it could be Lyme because I’ve been so tired and achy
Sometimes my hand pain feels like it shoots up the arm, so I worried it was a pinched nerve in my neck
noise: Occasional heartburn after spicy foods
Seasonal allergies with sneezing and itchy eyes
A mild headache once or twice a week that goes away with water or rest
A small patch of dry skin on the shin that gets worse in winter

progressive_reveal.symptom: The finger catching/clicking is worse in the morning and after heavy hand use, and it hurts at the base of the finger in the palm.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks for details about the clicking/catching finger and what makes it better or worse.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Tenderness along the palm side of the fingers and wrist, like the tendons themselves are sore.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks the patient to point to where it hurts and whether the pain feels in the joints or along the fingers/palm.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers at night that wakes the patient up.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about numbness/tingling, nighttime symptoms, or hand symptoms with wrist position.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Dry, gritty eyes and a dry mouth that makes swallowing dry foods hard.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about eye and mouth dryness, dental issues, or needing water to swallow.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Shortness of breath with stairs that has been worsening beyond the usual baseline from their lung condition.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about exercise tolerance changes, breathing symptoms, and compares to known pulmonary hypertension baseline.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Needing to stop to catch breath during sex and feeling embarrassed about it.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks sensitively about how symptoms affect daily life, including intimacy, using normalizing language and privacy.

general_concerns: Believes rheumatoid arthritis is just 'wear-and-tear' like osteoarthritis and that using her hands at work is the main cause.
Thinks the finger tendon swelling and clicking is from 'trapped fluid' that should be massaged out, and that rest alone will cure it.
Worries that starting stronger medicines means the disease is 'terminal' or that she will inevitably end up in a wheelchair.
Believes steroid injections or pills are always unsafe and will 'eat away the bones' after even one or two doses.
Thinks biologic or immune-targeting medicines are basically the same as chemotherapy and will make her constantly sick from infections.
Assumes pain level always matches joint damage, so if pain improves she believes inflammation is gone and medications can be stopped.
economic_concerns: Fears she cannot afford ongoing lab monitoring or specialist visits and may skip appointments if costs are unclear.
Worries that medications for rheumatoid arthritis are 'thousands of dollars' and assumes insurance will deny them, so she delays discussing options.
Concerned that time off work for infusions/appointments will risk losing hours or her job, so she prefers 'quick fixes' even if less effective.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, vague grocery cashier. Start with: “My fingers and wrists have been aching and stiff, and my ring finger keeps catching when I bend it.” Early in the visit, volunteer: morning stiffness in both hands lasting over an hour, swelling/soreness in both wrists and several knuckles, clicking/catching ring finger with palm pain, and trouble opening jars/turning keys due to weak, sore hands.

If asked, add: worse catching in the morning/after heavy use, tendon-like tenderness on palm side, warmth in small joints during flares, pain in the balls of your feet when first standing, weeks of unusual fatigue, and more shortness of breath on stairs.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: nighttime numbness/tingling in thumb/index/middle fingers, dropping objects, dry gritty eyes/dry mouth, firm painless elbow bumps, exertional chest tightness relieved by rest.

Resist discussing sex-related breathlessness and using extra leftover pain pills; minimize at first.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe carpal tunnel,” “gout,” “Lyme,” or “a pinched neck nerve.” If met with empathy, soften, admit worry about costs, missing work, and fear RA meds are unaffordable/denied; ask for “quick fixes.”

medications: Warfarin 5 mg PO nightly (dose adjusted to INR goal 2-3)
Riociguat 1.5 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
comorbidities: History of pulmonary embolism requiring long-term anticoagulation
allergies: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) — history of gastrointestinal bleeding while anticoagulated
confounders: Chronic anticoagulation with warfarin complicates use of NSAIDs and corticosteroid injections (bleeding risk) and can affect inflammatory marker interpretation if bleeding/anemia present
CTEPH-related dyspnea and fatigue can overlap with systemic inflammatory symptoms and limit exercise tolerance, confounding assessment of rheumatoid disease activity
Anemia of chronic disease can mimic or mask other causes of fatigue (e.g., iron deficiency from occult bleeding on anticoagulation)
Tenosynovitis symptoms may be confused with overuse tendinopathy or carpal tunnel syndrome; median nerve compression may coexist
Proton pump inhibitor use and chronic illness may contribute to low magnesium or B12 over time, potentially confounding neuropathic symptoms

required_evidence: Digital flexor tenosynovitis consistent with inflammatory synovitis
Positive anti-CCP IgG and/or rheumatoid factor
Elevated ESR and/or CRP
CBC to assess anemia/inflammation baseline before DMARD therapy

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal or mildly low (anemia of chronic disease possible)

medications: methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly
folic acid 1 mg PO daily
prednisone 10 mg PO daily (short course, taper over 2-4 weeks)
non_pharmacological: Hand/wrist splinting and activity modification for tenosynovitis
Occupational/hand therapy range-of-motion and tendon gliding exercises
Vaccination review prior to immunosuppression (influenza, pneumococcal, shingles as appropriate)
Avoid NSAIDs given prior GI bleeding while anticoagulated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks for symptom response and medication tolerance; reassess disease activity at 6-12 weeks for DMARD adjustment

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies rheumatoid arthritis (including tenosynovitis as a manifestation) as the working diagnosis to justify disease-modifying therapy rather than symptomatic-only treatment.

medications.name: methotrexate
medications.instructions: Take once weekly on the same day each week; do not take daily.

medications.name: folic acid
medications.instructions: Daily supplementation to reduce methotrexate adverse effects.

monitoring: baseline CBC, CMP (AST/ALT, albumin, creatinine), hepatitis B/C screening, pregnancy test if applicable
repeat CBC/CMP every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 8-12 weeks once stable
counsel on infection risk and to report fever, mouth sores, dyspnea/cough
counseling: avoid alcohol or minimize due to hepatotoxicity risk
avoid pregnancy; use effective contraception; discuss teratogenicity
avoid NSAIDs unless explicitly reviewed for safety (interaction/toxicity risk with methotrexate)

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates guideline-consistent first-line DMARD therapy (methotrexate with folate and appropriate monitoring) while demonstrating required pre-prescribing safety checks and key counseling, including avoidance of NSAIDs per benchmark requirement.

nl_assertions: Before any prescribing, the agent explicitly performs: allergy_check, contraindication_check (e.g., pregnancy, liver disease, significant renal impairment, active serious infection), and drug_interaction_check (including other folate antagonists, hepatotoxic drugs, and NSAIDs). These checks are treated as critical; omission is a fail.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the exact terms “rheumatoid arthritis” and “tenosynovitis,” and links symptoms (inflammatory pain, stiffness, swelling) to an autoimmune inflammatory process.
The agent explains the treatment plan and includes the exact terms “methotrexate” and “avoid NSAIDs,” with clear rationale (disease modification vs symptomatic relief; safety/interaction concerns).
The agent provides safe methotrexate instructions emphasizing once-weekly dosing to prevent overdose errors.

The agent includes folic acid co-prescription (or an equivalent folate supplementation plan) and outlines baseline and ongoing lab monitoring (CBC, LFTs, renal function) as part of safe DMARD initiation.

The agent screens for/addresses pregnancy potential and contraception due to methotrexate teratogenicity; if pregnancy is present or planned, methotrexate is not prescribed and the agent pivots to an alternative plan.
The agent discusses red-flag adverse effects and return precautions (infection symptoms, oral ulcers, dyspnea/cough suggesting pneumonitis, abnormal bleeding/bruising).

The agent avoids unsafe or non-guideline therapy choices for RA as first-line monotherapy (e.g., chronic opioids, antibiotics) and does not present NSAIDs as primary therapy given the required milestone to avoid them.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose RA with tenosynovitis and start methotrexate with folate plus required safety checks and monitoring.
must_collect: pattern of joint involvement (symmetric small joints, swelling)
duration of symptoms and morning stiffness
tenosynovitis symptoms/signs (tendon sheath pain, triggering, swelling)
current medications (including NSAIDs, TMP-SMX, warfarin, anticonvulsants) and supplements
steps: Explain diagnosis: rheumatoid arthritis with tenosynovitis; discuss inflammatory autoimmune nature and typical course.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing.
Perform contraindication_check before prescribing (pregnancy, severe hepatic/renal disease, active serious infection, significant cytopenias).
Perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing (notably NSAIDs, TMP-SMX, other hepatotoxic or marrow-suppressive agents).
Prescribe methotrexate once weekly plus folic acid; provide clear dosing instructions and safety counseling including avoid NSAIDs.
Arrange baseline labs and monitoring schedule; provide return precautions for adverse effects.

acceptable_variants.description: Methotrexate initiated subcutaneously instead of oral due to GI intolerance risk or preference, with the same safety checks, folate, and monitoring; still includes explicit 'avoid NSAIDs' counseling.
steps: Explain diagnosis: rheumatoid arthritis with tenosynovitis.
Complete allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check before prescribing.
Prescribe methotrexate subcutaneous once weekly plus folic acid; counsel on administration and adverse effects; include 'avoid NSAIDs'.
Order/arrange baseline and follow-up CBC/CMP and relevant screening; provide return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: If methotrexate is contraindicated (e.g., pregnancy, severe liver disease), the agent does NOT prescribe methotrexate; instead documents RA diagnosis and prescribes an alternative DMARD appropriate to the contraindication while maintaining the required safety checks and communication milestones (must still mention methotrexate as not suitable).
steps: Explain diagnosis: rheumatoid arthritis with tenosynovitis.
Complete allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check before prescribing.
Explain that methotrexate is not appropriate due to a specific contraindication; discuss risks/benefits.
Prescribe an alternative appropriate DMARD (e.g., hydroxychloroquine or sulfasalazine depending on context) with necessary monitoring; include 'avoid NSAIDs' counseling per benchmark requirement.
Provide follow-up and safety-net instructions.
